summary:
The origins of glassmaking in the ancient Near East are debated, with some scholars placing the earliest deliberate production of glass objects in Mesopotamia or Egypt as early as the third millennium BCE. However, the precursor technology of glazing — applying a glassy surface coating to ceramic, stone, or faience objects — clearly predates true glass production. Faience, a sintered quartz material with a glass-like surface, was produced in Egypt and Mesopotamia from at least 3500 BCE onward and may represent the technological pathway through which true glass was eventually discovered.

The earliest small glass objects — typically beads and pendants — appear in the archaeological record of Mesopotamia and Egypt around 2500–2000 BCE. It is possible that glass was first observed as a byproduct of high-temperature copper smelting or faience production, and that artisans gradually learned to work it deliberately. True vessel glass does not appear until roughly 1500 BCE in the Near East. The development of glassworking illustrates how the high-temperature crafts — metallurgy, pottery, and faience production — that flourished during the [[event:1617|Uruk period]] created the technical knowledge base from which later technologies emerged.
